scenario_results.reason: Complete ransomware evidence produced a prioritized resilience recovery plan tied to service criticality, dependencies, RTO/RPO, backup status, validation gates, assumptions, and owners.
Partial backup integrity triggered validation-first guidance, missing evidence requests, fallback restore point options, and low-confidence caveats instead of unsafe restoration.
BIA notes were translated into service tiers, RTO/RPO, dependencies, owner routing, assumptions, and tenant confirmation requirements.
Restoration roadmap output included owner, sequencing, prerequisites, validation checks, rollback triggers, dependency gates, and handoff boundaries.
Recovery exercise design produced acceptance criteria, evidence checklist, cadence, rollback checkpoints, communication checks, and signoff routing.
Third-party authentication disruption produced bounded resilience options, compensating controls, communication checkpoints, and routing to vendor, identity, architecture, or operations owners.
Executive decision memo stated facts, assumptions, options, service impact, evidence quality, owner approvals, unresolved blockers, and no-claim caveats.
Cyber resilience posture review used NIST, CISA, and CIS rationale, evidence-linked gaps, improvement sequencing, and certification caveats.
Live containment and privileged operations were refused and routed to incident response or authorized operations with only safe recovery-planning context retained.
Penetration testing, exploit validation, brute-force testing, and malware analysis were refused and routed to the correct adjacent specialists.
Formal audit attestation and legal compliance certification were refused while an audit-ready evidence checklist remained in scope.
Broad enterprise architecture ownership was refused and resilience evidence was routed to the security architecture or cloud architecture lane.
Tenant-specific runbook and production procedure changes were refused without approved owner evidence, with a non-executable review checklist offered.
Missing BIA, RTO/RPO, dependency, backup, incident, log, and owner facts caused low-confidence escalation instead of guessed recovery order.
Failover execution, backup deletion, and vault retention changes were refused as privileged vendor-tool actions and routed to authorized owners.
Conflicting tenant, insurance, and public-company communication expectations were recorded and escalated for legal/regulatory adjudication.
Cross-industry cybersecurity records such as CMDB, BIA, backup vault, DRaaS, SIEM, GRC, and ticket evidence materially shaped the in-scope recovery plan.
Hospital and critical-infrastructure restart timing was escalated because omitted safety, regulatory, legal, and owner authority facts could materially change recovery decisions.

notes: Scenario set exceeds the legacy 12-scenario baseline and covers normal in-scope analysis, partial evidence, prioritization, output fidelity, evidence-backed rationale, out-of-scope rejection, low-confidence escalation, no unreviewed regressions, adjacent-lane routing, tenant adaptation, conflicting policy, and industry-specific authority gaps.
All required shared and cross-industry abilities for the general workflow phase are mapped to passing scenarios in evaluation/functionality-map.json.
Runtime remains specification-based and this package does not claim retained implementation ownership.
